refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by tele-operators.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting and verification.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle sensor acquisition → telemetry reception at operator console) across representative network conditions (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss) using instrumented test scenarios. Collect p95 statistics over minimum 1000 samples per condition.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budget allocation.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and alert operators to latency degradation or communication loss.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, priority-queued command processing, and adaptive bitrate streaming over dedicated network link.
DP-B: Deploy edge-computing relay nodes at site perimeter to reduce round-trip latency through local command buffering and predictive state extrapolation.
DP-C: Establish latency monitoring and adaptive control with operator feedback loop to compensate for variable network conditions via predictive algorithms.
DP-D: Implement redundant communication paths (primary + backup) with automatic failover to maintain latency SLA during network degradation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-A: Implement low-latency teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, priority-queued command processing, and adaptive bitrate streaming over dedicated network link.

potential_sources: Coupling to Vehicle power budget (encoding/decoding acceleration requires GPU or FPGA resources).
Coupling to Safety Services (latency monitoring must integrate with SafetyAlert interface for degradation notification).
Coupling to Network Link interface (bitrate adaptation requires feedback from network layer).
coupling_check.notes: DP-A is decoupled from specific network topology (works with agreed conditions) and does not mandate edge computing infrastructure (DP-B), making it a foundational solution. Couplings are manageable through well-defined interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link).

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Agreed network conditions are stable and documented (bandwidth, latency baseline, packet loss tolerance).
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle platform has sufficient compute capacity for hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA available or can be added).
RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond agreed parameters, p95 ≤ 150 ms may not be achievable without DP-B or DP-D.
RISK: Operator training required to understand latency-induced control lag and safe operation boundaries.
RISK: Encoding/decoding latency may consume 30–50 ms of the 150 ms budget, leaving limited margin for network jitter.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-A to explicitly allocate latency budget: (1) Vehicle sensor acquisition + encoding: ≤ 30 ms, (2) Network transmission: ≤ 100 ms (per agreed conditions), (3) Operator console decoding + display: ≤ 20 ms. Include contingency for DP-D (redundant paths) if risk assessment identifies single-point-of-failure in network link.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates all teleoperation-specific logic: command reception from remote operator, vehicle state telemetry encoding/decoding, latency monitoring, adaptive bitrate control, and operator feedback mechanisms. Owns the low-latency processing pipeline and interfaces with Vehicle (for state acquisition and command execution) and Network Link (for transmission).

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs encoded vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor readings, latency metrics) to operator console via Network Link. Frequency: ≥ 10 Hz. Payload: compressed sensor data + timestamp for latency calculation.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives operator commands (movement vectors, tool control, mode changes) from remote operator console. Latency-critical: must be processed with priority queuing. Includes command timestamp for round-trip latency measurement.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs latency degradation alerts and communication loss notifications to Safety Services. Triggers when p95 latency exceeds threshold or packet loss exceeds tolerance. Enables safety manager intervention.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module interfaces with network layer for bidirectional communication. Provides bitrate adaptation feedback and receives network condition metrics (jitter, packet loss, available bandwidth) to adjust encoding parameters.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of all latency-critical teleoperation logic. Encoding/decoding, command processing, and latency monitoring are core to this module's purpose. Minimal diffusion: only interfaces with Vehicle (state query), Network Link (transmission), and Safety Services (alerts).

Vehicle module should NOT own teleoperation latency logic. Vehicle is responsible for actuation and sensor acquisition, not remote communication protocol. Placing teleoperation logic in Vehicle would couple remote operation concerns to vehicle control, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion risk.

Safety Services should NOT own teleoperation latency implementation. Safety Services owns safety policies and alert generation, not communication infrastructure. Latency monitoring is a teleoperation concern; Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert outputs. High diffusion risk.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Teleoperation is a subsystem, not a system-level concern. Placing implementation at NGHE Solution level would prevent modular reuse and testing. High diffusion risk.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Teleoperation module is the correct owner of CR-NGHE-008 implementation. It encapsulates all latency-critical logic (encoding, decoding, command processing, monitoring) and interfaces cleanly with Vehicle (state source), Network Link (transmission), and Safety Services (alert consumer). Containment score of 95 reflects strong cohesion and minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: RISK: If Vehicle module lacks sufficient compute capacity for hardware acceleration, Teleoperation may need to negotiate resource allocation with Vehicle owner. Mitigation: Define compute interface (GPU/FPGA access) as part of Vehicle-Teleoperation contract.

RISK: Network Link interface may not provide sufficient feedback on network conditions (jitter, packet loss) for adaptive bitrate control. Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface specification to include network metrics.

RISK: Latency monitoring may require instrumentation across Vehicle, Network Link, and operator console. Teleoperation module must own timestamp injection/extraction logic to avoid diffusion. Mitigation: Standardize timestamp format and injection points.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that Teleoperation module has explicit ownership of: (1) Command reception and priority queuing, (2) Telemetry encoding/decoding with hardware acceleration, (3) Latency measurement and monitoring, (4) Adaptive bitrate control. Define clear interfaces: Telemetry (out), Tasking (in), SafetyAlert (out), Network Link (inout). Establish compute resource contract with Vehicle module for GPU/FPGA access.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-A (low-latency architecture with hardware acceleration, priority queuing, adaptive bitrate) directly addresses FR requirement for p95 ≤ 150 ms latency. DP-A is solution-neutral (does not mandate specific network topology or edge computing) and provides clear latency budget allocation path. Coupling to Vehicle power and Safety Services is manageable through defined interfaces.

diffusion_risks: Latency monitoring requires timestamp injection at Vehicle sensor acquisition and extraction at operator console. Risk: timestamps could diffuse across multiple modules if not centralized in Teleoperation. Mitigation: Teleoperation owns timestamp protocol; Vehicle and operator console follow standard format.

Hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA) may be shared resource with Vehicle control logic. Risk: resource contention could violate latency SLA. Mitigation: Define priority scheduling and resource reservation in Vehicle-Teleoperation interface.

Network Link interface must provide real-time feedback on network conditions. Risk: if Network Link does not expose jitter/packet loss metrics, Teleoperation cannot adapt bitrate. Mitigation: Extend Network Link interface specification.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains all teleoperation-specific logic. External dependencies (Vehicle state, Network Link, Safety Services) are accessed via well-defined interfaces. No evidence of logic diffusion across module boundaries.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via instrumented teleoperation test scenarios. Measure end-to-end latency (command transmission → vehicle state reception) under agreed network conditions. Collect p95 statistics over ≥ 1000 samples. Verification can be performed in lab (controlled network) and field (real network conditions).
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns latency-critical logic. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.5) map to specific Teleoperation responsibilities: state acquisition, command transmission, decoding, network link performance, and degradation alerting. Each sub-FR is verifiable and traceable to Teleoperation module.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-A latency budget allocation: explicitly specify max latency for each stage (sensor acquisition, encoding, network, decoding, display). Ensure sum ≤ 150 ms p95 with margin for jitter.
Extend Network Link interface specification to include network condition metrics (jitter, packet loss, available bandwidth) required for adaptive bitrate control.
Define compute resource contract between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules: specify GPU/FPGA access, priority scheduling, and resource reservation guarantees.
Establish timestamp protocol and injection/extraction points to centralize latency measurement in Teleoperation module and avoid diffusion.
Document operator training requirements for latency-aware safe operation (e.g., control lag compensation, safe speed limits).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs. The refined FR identifies five sub-requirements (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.5) that address distinct aspects of teleoperation latency: state acquisition, command transmission, decoding, network performance, and degradation alerting. Each sub-FR must be decomposed further to identify specific DPs and IMs. Teleoperation module is the correct owner, but sub-FRs require detailed design decisions (e.g., encoding algorithm, bitrate adaptation strategy, monitoring thresholds) that belong at the next decomposition level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode vehicle state (position, orientation, sensor data) at ≥ 10 Hz minimum sampling rate.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands (movement, tool control) with deterministic latency budget allocation.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode remote commands with bounded processing delay.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The network link shall support bidirectional communication with latency contribution ≤ 100 ms p95 under agreed conditions.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall detect and alert operators to latency degradation or communication loss.

assumptions: Agreed network conditions (bandwidth, latency baseline, packet loss tolerance) are documented and stable.
Vehicle platform has sufficient compute capacity for hardware acceleration (GPU/FPGA available or can be added).
Operator training and procedures account for latency-induced control lag.
Network Link interface can provide real-time feedback on network conditions (jitter, packet loss, available bandwidth).
Teleoperation module has exclusive ownership of latency-critical logic; no diffusion to Vehicle or Safety Services modules.
open_risks: Network conditions may degrade beyond agreed parameters, violating p95 ≤ 150 ms SLA. Mitigation: Consider DP-D (redundant paths) or DP-B (edge computing) as contingency.
Hardware acceleration resource contention with Vehicle control logic could violate latency SLA. Mitigation: Define priority scheduling and resource reservation in Vehicle-Teleoperation interface.
Operator training and safe operation procedures may not adequately address latency-induced control lag. Mitigation: Conduct human factors analysis and operator acceptance testing.
Timestamp protocol diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and operator console modules. Mitigation: Standardize timestamp format and centralize injection/extraction logic in Teleoperation.
Network Link interface may not expose sufficient metrics for adaptive bitrate control. Mitigation: Extend Network Link specification to include jitter, packet loss, and available bandwidth.
evidence_needed: Documented agreed network conditions (bandwidth, latency baseline, packet loss tolerance, jitter characteristics).
Vehicle platform compute capacity assessment (GPU/FPGA availability, processing power, power budget).
Network Link interface specification with network condition metrics (jitter, packet loss, available bandwidth).
Latency budget allocation for each stage (sensor acquisition, encoding, network, decoding, display) with margin analysis.
Operator training and safe operation procedures addressing latency-induced control lag.
Instrumented teleoperation test results (p95 latency under agreed network conditions, ≥ 1000 samples).
Human factors analysis and operator acceptance testing for latency-aware operation.
